key_insight: Accurate finite-element resolution of rapidly rotating Gross–Pitaevskii ground states requires much finer meshes than the vortex-core scale alone suggests, because local energy flatness controls whether discrete vortices are captured correctly.
engineering_relevance: For Bose–Einstein condensate simulations, the result gives a practical mesh-design rule to avoid missing vortex structures and to predict when discretization error will decay like h/ε².
method: Analyze ε-dependent ground states in the rapid-rotation Thomas-Fermi scaling.
Derive mesh-size conditions ensuring discrete ground states are quasi-best approximations of the continuous one.
Relate the required resolution to the first spectral gap of the Riemannian Hessian at the ground state.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical finite-element analysis and asymptotic error bounds, including an absolute H¹-error scaling of h/ε² under sufficiently strong mesh-resolution conditions.
watch_item: The stronger-than-natural mesh constraint may be expensive in 2D/3D vortex simulations, so coarse meshes can appear reasonable while still failing to resolve the correct discrete ground state.
paper_title: Numerical vortex resolution for the Gross-Pitaevskii equation in the rapid rotation Thomas-Fermi scaling
